items.title: Model no longer imports upward from a controller (§7.1)
items.precondition: Run dependency-cruiser's mechanized models-no-upward-imports rule on the branch head and on the pre-fix baseline.
steps: Stash the fix and run `npm run depcruise` against the original commit 3c8aa614.
Restore the fix and re-run `npm run depcruise`.
Compare the models-no-upward-imports violation count and the offending edges.
items.expected: The TasteProfileModel -> service.taste-profile edge disappears; the rule's violation count drops by exactly one and no new edge appears.
items.evidence: Baseline: 7 models-no-upward-imports violations (39 total), including `src/models/website-builder/TasteProfileModel.ts -> src/controllers/admin-websites/feature-services/service.taste-profile.ts`. After: 6 violations (38 total), TasteProfileModel absent. depcruise reports 0 errors both times.
items.notes: Persisted shapes moved to the neutral src/types/tasteProfile.ts (precedent: types/adminReset.ts, types/pmsMapping.ts). The 6 remaining violations are pre-existing and out of scope for this PR.

items.title: Org A cannot READ org B's taste profile by id (§11.7)
items.precondition: In-memory taste_profiles table whose query builder actually applies the model's .where() conditions (mirrors PracticeFactModel.test.ts).
steps: Seed a single row owned by org B (id tp-b).
Call TasteProfileModel.findByIdForOrg('tp-b', ORG_A) - org A holding a leaked or guessed uuid.
items.expected: Returns undefined; org B's row is never disclosed, and 'wrong org' is indistinguishable from 'missing' so no existence information leaks.
items.evidence: src/__tests__/TasteProfileModel.test.ts - "org A CANNOT read org B's profile even with the exact row id" passed; a sibling test asserts the WHERE clause contains { id, organization_id }.
items.notes: findLatestByOrgAndLocation was already correctly scoped and was left unchanged; a regression test covers it.

items.title: Org A cannot APPROVE org B's taste profile (§11.7)
items.precondition: Seed one draft row owned by org B.
steps: Call TasteProfileModel.markApproved('tp-b', ORG_A, 'owner@org-a.test').
Inspect the victim row.
items.expected: 0 rows updated; org B's row stays status='draft' with approved_by null - a cross-tenant owner sign-off is impossible.
items.evidence: src/__tests__/TasteProfileModel.test.ts - "org A CANNOT approve org B's profile" passed; the positive case "markApproved DOES approve the caller's own profile" also passed, proving the scope is not simply blocking everything.
items.notes: markApproved is not a BaseModel static, so organizationId could be added as a required 2nd positional argument directly.

items.title: Org A cannot DELETE org B's taste profile (§11.7)
items.precondition: Seed rows owned by org A and org B.
steps: Call TasteProfileModel.deleteByIdForOrg('tp-b', ORG_A).
Call deleteByIdForOrg('tp-a', ORG_A) for the positive case.
items.expected: The cross-org delete removes 0 rows and org B's row survives; the same-org delete removes exactly 1 row and leaves the other tenant untouched.
items.evidence: src/__tests__/TasteProfileModel.test.ts - "org A CANNOT delete org B's profile" and "deleteByIdForOrg DOES delete the caller's own profile" both passed.
items.notes: An unscoped delete-by-id was the most destructive of the three holes.

items.title: The isolation suite is non-vacuous (mutation-tested)
items.precondition: A passing isolation suite proves nothing if its assertions cannot fail. Mutate the model to remove the tenant scope and re-run.
steps: Strip `organization_id: organizationId` from all three scoped WHERE clauses in TasteProfileModel.
Re-run src/__tests__/TasteProfileModel.test.ts.
Restore the file and re-run tsc plus the suite.
items.expected: The mutant fails exactly the isolation assertions - the tests detect the vulnerability rather than passing regardless.
items.evidence: Mutant run: 5 failed | 5 passed (10). The three "org A CANNOT ..." tests plus the WHERE-shape and markApproved tests failed. After restore: tsc exit 0 and 10/10 passed.
items.notes: This is why the mock APPLIES .where() filters instead of only asserting query shape - a shape assertion passes even if the clause filters nothing.

items.title: The tenant scope cannot be forgotten at compile time (§11.7)
items.precondition: BaseModel exposes unscoped findById/deleteById statics that TasteProfileModel would otherwise inherit.
steps: Attempt to widen findById with a required organizationId parameter and run tsc.
Seal the unscoped entry points instead and re-run tsc.
Attempt `TasteProfileModel.findById('tp-b')`.
items.expected: Widening is impossible (TS2417: class static side incorrectly extends base class static side). With the seal, an unscoped call is a compile error (TS2554: Expected 0 arguments, but got 1), and a runtime throw backstops untyped callers.
items.evidence: TS2417 reproduced on a minimal repro before the design was chosen; TS2554 confirmed on the seal. `npx tsc --noEmit` exits 0 on the branch. The runtime throw is covered by "seals the unscoped BaseModel entry points rather than inheriting them".
items.notes: This is why the fix is a seal plus scoped siblings (findByIdForOrg / deleteByIdForOrg) rather than an extra argument on findById itself - the review's literal wording is not expressible in TypeScript here. Repo analog: GbpWorkItemModel.findByIdForScope.

items.title: Layer-2: compose -> persist end-to-end against the running app
items.precondition: BLOCKED. Requires a production entry point that composes a profile and persists it - a route, job, or consumer running against the app.
steps: Trigger a taste-profile compose for a real organization/location through a production surface.
Confirm the gated profile is persisted to taste_profiles under the caller's organization_id.
Confirm an owner can approve only their own profile through that surface.
items.expected: Not executable on this branch. composeTasteProfile() and composeFromExtractors() are called only by taste-profile-composition.test.ts, and TasteProfileModel has zero importers repo-wide - there is no running surface to drive.
items.evidence: Verified by grep on the branch head: the only callers of the composition functions are in src/__tests__/taste-profile-composition.test.ts; no src/ module imports TasteProfileModel.
items.notes: This is review finding #3 restated as an acceptance gap. It is recorded as pending - NOT waived to green - so the package rolls up 'In Progress' rather than 'Passed'. Substituting the Layer-1 unit runs for Layer-2 here would be exactly the over-claim the honesty gate exists to prevent.
items.waiver: WAIVED as out of scope for this PR, per Dave's round-3 review (2026-07-16T19:44:56Z): "Because this branch deliberately has no production caller, either scope the artifact as an internal-only foundation with an honest N/A behavioral item, or wire/run the claimed surface." This artifact takes the first option: PR #160 is scoped as an internal-only foundation (model + migration + composition, zero importers), so a Layer-2 behavioral run is N/A here - not deferred, not skipped, but genuinely not expressible until a consuming surface exists. NOTE: the live-PostgreSQL waiver does NOT cover this item and is not claimed for it - T7 would be unrunnable even with a live database, because no route, job, or consumer composes and persists a profile. The blocker is the absent caller, not the absent DB. This item must be executed by the PR that wires the surface (the stacked #171), against the running app; it is recorded here rather than deleted so that obligation cannot be lost.

items.title: No regression across the backend suite
items.precondition: Node 22.23.1 (Node 20 throws ERR_REQUIRE_ESM in vitest). Run the full suite on the branch head.
steps: Run `npx tsc --noEmit`.
Run `npm test`.
Run `npm run check:conventions -- --strict`, `npm run lint`, `npm run depcruise`.
items.expected: Typecheck clean, no failing tests, no new structural violations.
items.evidence: tsc --noEmit exit 0. npm test: 62 files / 481 tests passed, 0 failed. check:conventions --strict: 0 CLEAR structural violations, exit 0. lint: 0 errors (2384 pre-existing warnings). depcruise: 0 errors, 38 warnings (39 pre-fix).
items.notes: The PR body's claim that vitest will not run in-env (a pre-existing ESM/toolchain issue) did not reproduce on Node 22.23.1 - the committed vitest files run normally.

items.title: Current GitHub checks attached to the PR head (review finding 4, part 3)
items.precondition: NOT SATISFIABLE ON ANY BRANCH. The review asked for tenant-isolation tests (T2-T5), the acceptance artifacts (this file), and current GitHub checks. This item records the third part rather than letting it go unrecorded.
steps: Inspect every trigger in .github/workflows/.
Run `gh pr checks 160`.
items.expected: There is no CI to attach. The repo has only deploy workflows, each triggered by a PUSH to one branch - deploy-sandbox.yml on [sandbox], dev.yml on [dev/dave], main.yml on [main]. No pull_request trigger exists anywhere, so no workflow runs against a feature branch or a pull request, and `gh pr checks 160` can only ever report none.
items.evidence: Confirmed BOTH ways. Directly: `gh pr checks 160` returns "no checks reported on the 'claude/taste-profile-spine' branch" against PR head da282309. Structurally: `grep -rn pull_request .github/workflows/` returns nothing, and the three workflow files carry push-only triggers - deploy-sandbox.yml [sandbox], dev.yml [dev/dave], main.yml [main]. The structural proof is the load-bearing one: with no trigger, no check CAN exist to report, so this is not a transient empty result that would fill in later.
items.notes: HONEST LIMIT, not a gap this PR can close. This is an infrastructure absence already raised with Dave, not something the branch did wrong; adding a pull_request CI workflow is its own change, out of scope here. Recorded as pending rather than waived to green so the package cannot roll up 'Passed' on a check nobody ran. Every gate CI would have proven was instead run locally on Node 22.23.1 and is recorded in T8 with exit codes.
items.waiver: WAIVED as N/A on Dave's explicit round-2 ruling (2026-07-16T16:21:48Z): "The absence of PR CI itself is N/A, not a blocker." Structurally unsatisfiable on any branch - no pull_request trigger exists in .github/workflows/, so no check CAN attach to a PR head. Every gate CI would have run was run locally on Node 22.23.1 with exit codes recorded (T19). Kept as an item rather than deleted because the underlying infrastructure gap is real and still open; it is simply not this PR's to close.

items.title: Every inherited unscoped BaseModel entry point is sealed (§11.7/§5.5)
items.precondition: Rev 1 sealed findById/deleteById only. BaseModel also exposes condition-based reads, an id-based update, count, paginate, and createReturningId, whose WHERE clause is whatever the caller passes - so the isolation guarantee held only for callers who remembered to add an organization predicate. Full public surface enumerated from src/models/BaseModel.ts rather than from the review's list.
steps: Override findOne, findMany, updateById, count, paginate and createReturningId to take no arguments, so a real call is a compile error (TS2554); keep a runtime throw as the backstop for untyped callers.
Add cross-org tests asserting each sealed method throws AND that the other tenant's row is neither returned nor modified.
Mutation-check: stash the model fix and re-run the new tests against the pre-fix source.
Run `npx tsc --noEmit` with the @ts-expect-error directives in place.
items.expected: Each sealed entry point is unreachable at compile time; no cross-tenant row is read or mutated at runtime; the scoped siblings (findByIdForOrg / findLatestByOrgAndLocation / markApproved / deleteByIdForOrg) remain the only way in.
items.evidence: vitest: 43/43 passing across the two taste-profile suites (was 25/25). Mutation-verified by restoring the pre-fix model from HEAD~1 and re-running BOTH gates, which catch different halves of the seal. RUNTIME half - `npx vitest run` on the two suites: 7 failed | 36 passed (43); the failures are exactly the seal tests (findOne, findMany, updateById x2, count+paginate, createReturningId, and the §5.4 smuggling test). COMPILE half - `npx tsc --noEmit`: 7 errors, being 6x TS2578 'Unused @ts-expect-error directive' at the seal call sites plus 1x TS2322 on T11's type-level status assertion; i.e. those calls compiled before the fix and do not compile after it. Against the fixed model vitest is 43/43 and `tsc --noEmit` exits 0, which is only possible if every directive is firing. The model was restored byte-identical afterward (`git diff --quiet` clean).
items.notes: createReturningId was NOT in the review's list; it was found by enumerating BaseModel's public surface directly, and it is a write path that also bypassed create()'s draft-only contract. count() is the one honest exception to the compile-time rule: BaseModel.count() is callable with zero arguments, so its seal is enforced at runtime only - documented in the model. No countForOrg/lister was added because no caller needs one (YAGNI); the seals name the scoped replacement to add when one does. THE ENUMERATION, so 'every' is auditable rather than unbounded: BaseModel exposes 11 public statics - beginTransaction, count, create, createReturningId, deleteById, findById, findMany, findOne, paginate, transaction, updateById. 8 are sealed (count, createReturningId, deleteById, findById, findMany, findOne, paginate, updateById); create is replaced with the draft-only override (T11); the remaining 2 - transaction and beginTransaction - are deliberately NOT sealed because they only delegate to db.transaction() and return a handle, never querying this table, so there is no tenant predicate to enforce on them.

items.title: A profile can only be created as a draft; approval requires the owner sign-off (§5.4)
items.precondition: create() accepted an optional status and wrote it directly, so an approved row could be minted at insert time with no approved_by/approved_at - an unsigned approval that reads downstream exactly like a signed one.
steps: Remove status from create()'s input type and always write 'draft'; keep the explicit column copy so no extra column can ride in on the object.
Add a type-level assertion that 'status' is not in keyof Parameters<typeof create>[0].
Add a behavioural test that create() persists status=draft with a null sign-off stamp.
Add a test where an untyped caller smuggles status/approved_by/approved_at through a cast.
items.expected: status is not expressible in the create input; the persisted row is always a draft with a null approved_by/approved_at; the only route to 'approved' is markApproved(), which writes the status and the signature in one update.
items.evidence: vitest: the three §5.4 tests pass against the fixed model. Mutation-verified: against the pre-fix model the smuggling test fails and `tsc --noEmit` reports TS2322 'Type true is not assignable to type never' on the type-level assertion (status WAS in the input type). The mock query builder now records inserts, so status is asserted as WRITTEN, not as intended.
items.notes: The insert assertion runs against an in-memory table, not Postgres - it proves the column value the model sends, which is where the rule lives. markApproved()'s own cross-org coverage is unchanged (T3).

items.title: A review source is attached only to words that review actually said
items.precondition: resolveReviewSource's docstring promised 'we never fabricate a source', but it resolved a source on the reviewer NAME alone (never examining the quote), and its containment test ran in BOTH directions, so a long claim that merely wrapped a short review ('Great!') inherited that review's receipt. Both produced a clean, followable link in the audit trail for words the review never said - independently found and verified against the code, not reported by the review.
steps: Require one-directional containment: the quote must be a verbatim (whitespace-normalized) substring OF the review's text - the same structural check extractPracticeFacts.processor.verifyFacts already applies to fact excerpts.
Stop treating a reviewer name as evidence; use it only to disambiguate between real origins, or to veto a contradicting attribution.
Drop the claim when several reviews contain the quote and the name cannot narrow it to exactly one.
Add regression tests for each path, plus the honest cases that must still resolve.
Mutation-check against the pre-fix service.
items.expected: A source is returned only when exactly one review is provably the origin of the quote; every other case returns null and the gate drops the claim with reason 'no_source' - which is what the docstring already promised.
items.evidence: vitest: 7 new tests in 'a source is the origin, not a lookalike' pass against the fixed service. Mutation-verified: 5 of them fail against the pre-fix service (the name-only path, the wrapped-short-review path, the contradicting attribution, the ambiguous quote, and the name-as-disambiguator case). The two pre-existing resolution tests still pass unchanged, so honest sourced claims are not dropped.
items.notes: Ambiguity now drops the claim rather than picking the first origin: the words are real but which review said them is unknown, and a guess presented as a receipt is the failure this function exists to prevent.

items.title: Only recognized forms of receipt count as a source
items.precondition: isRealSource checked string presence and a non-empty payload only, so any invented label (an arbitrary `field: value` pair) counted as provenance - an unfollowable citation that reads in the audit trail exactly like a followable one.
steps: Restrict to the three forms the system actually produces: review:<id>, an http(s) URL, or a labeled excerpt whose field is one of the PracticeFactSourceField values (business_data / page_content / post_content).
Fail closed on anything unrecognized.
Add unit tests for each accepted and rejected form, plus an end-to-end drop through the gate.
items.expected: Recognized forms pass; an invented label, a bare reference with no payload, and free prose are all rejected, so the claim is dropped with reason 'no_source' rather than carrying a citation that points at nothing.
items.evidence: vitest: the two new isRealSource tests pass against the fixed util and fail against the pre-fix util (mutation-verified). All pre-existing isRealSource expectations still pass unchanged. An unrecognized practice-fact source_field is now dropped by the gate as a consequence, with no separate filter in the adapter.
items.notes: The field vocabulary is mirrored from PracticeFactModel.PracticeFactSourceField rather than imported, to keep this gate a dependency-free pure scanner; the model is named in a keep-in-sync comment. Adding a genuinely new source kind is now a deliberate edit here alongside the code that emits it.

items.title: fact_text is supported by its own source_excerpt
items.precondition: OPEN LIMIT, recorded rather than closed. The composition layer attaches a receipt to each practice fact but never checks that fact_text - a distilled restatement, not a verbatim span - is actually supported by the excerpt it cites. A fact could overstate its excerpt and still pass, caught only if the overstatement trips enforceHonesty's banned language.
steps: Verify where the excerpt is checked today: extractPracticeFacts.processor.verifyFacts confirms it is verbatim against the real scraped/GBP input.
Determine whether the composition layer can re-derive that support from its own inputs.
items.expected: Support for a restatement is verified where the source text lives (the extractor), which is the only layer that holds the original input.
items.evidence: Verified by reading the code: the excerpt IS verified verbatim upstream (extractPracticeFacts.processor.ts verifyFacts, which also requires source_field to be a known PracticeFactSourceField). The composition service receives only the extractor's output, so it cannot re-derive whether the restatement is supported. The boundary is now documented at the practice-fact mapping in service.taste-profile.ts.
items.notes: Deliberately NOT closed with a heuristic. A token-overlap test would pass almost any restatement reusing a couple of the excerpt's words, so it would not catch the overstatement it appears to guard against - it would only make an unverified claim LOOK verified, which is the exact failure this gate exists to prevent. Closing it for real belongs in the extractor, where the source text is. Recorded as pending so the package cannot roll up 'Passed' on a check nobody can run here.
items.waiver: WAIVED as an accepted upstream limitation, per Dave's round-3 review (2026-07-16T19:44:56Z): "If T14 is an accepted upstream limitation, record an explicit waiver rather than leaving finalization ambiguous." It is one. Support for a distilled restatement can only be checked where the original source text lives - extractPracticeFacts.processor.verifyFacts, which already verifies the excerpt verbatim against the real scraped/GBP input. The composition layer receives only the extractor's OUTPUT, so it cannot re-derive that support no matter what this PR does; closing it here would require a token-overlap heuristic that would pass almost any restatement reusing a few of the excerpt's words - making an unverified claim LOOK verified, the exact failure the honesty gate exists to prevent. The boundary is documented in code at the practice-fact mapping in service.taste-profile.ts. Closing it for real is an extractor change, out of scope for this branch. Waived deliberately, NOT converted to pass: nothing here proves fact_text is supported by its excerpt, and this artifact does not claim it does.

items.title: No regression across the backend suite (round 2)
items.precondition: Node 22.23.1, `npm ci` in the /tmp/fix-160 worktree. There is NO live database in this environment (no Postgres, no Docker), so every DB-backed proof stays pending - see T7.
steps: npx tsc --noEmit
npx vitest run
npm run check:conventions -- --strict
items.expected: All three gates green with no new violations attributable to this branch.
items.evidence: tsc --noEmit exit 0. vitest: 62 files / 499 tests passed (up from 481; +18 from this round's regression tests). check:conventions --strict exit 0, 'CLEAR structural violations - backend (size + console + db): 0'; no taste-profile file appears in ANY check, including the §11.7 tenant-scope heuristic. File sizes within §2.4 tiers (model 272, service 470, util 227); resolveReviewSource is 41 lines (§2.2) at nesting depth 3 (§2.3).
items.notes: Local runs on Node 22.23.1, not CI - no pull_request workflow exists to attach checks to the PR head (T9). These are the same gates CI would have run.

items.title: The owner approval signature is write-once - a second approval cannot overwrite it (§5.4)
items.precondition: Round-3 finding #1. markApproved() filtered only by { id, organization_id }, so calling it on an ALREADY-APPROVED row re-ran the UPDATE and overwrote approved_by/approved_at. A later caller in the same org could silently reattribute the owner sign-off to a different person and time. The approval gate is the point where a human stakes what the AI drafted; a signature that can be overwritten is not a signature, and the audit trail would name someone who never approved.
steps: Add `status: "draft"` to markApproved()'s WHERE clause, making draft -> approved a one-way transition: an approved row stops matching its own update predicate.
Test the transition BOTH ways: the first approval records the signature; a second approver (same org, same id, correct scope) cannot rewrite it.
Test that the SAME owner re-approving is an idempotent no-op that does not restamp approved_at.
Test that the guard lives in the WHERE clause the DB evaluates, not in caller-side code.
Test that the guard is POSITIVE (status = draft) and so fails closed on any future status.
Mutation-check: strip the guard and re-run.
items.expected: First approval returns 1 and stamps approved_by/approved_at. Every subsequent call returns 0 and leaves the original approver and timestamp untouched.
items.evidence: vitest exit 0, 27/27 in src/__tests__/TasteProfileModel.test.ts (was 19). MUTATION-VERIFIED and non-vacuous: with `status: "draft"` stripped from the WHERE, `npx vitest run` on this suite exits 1 with 6 failed | 21 passed (27). The decisive failure is "a SECOND approver cannot rewrite the original signature" - AssertionError: expected 1 to be +0, i.e. WITHOUT the fix the re-approval updates the row, which is the reported bug reproduced. Also failing on the mutant: the idempotent re-approval test, the WHERE-shape test, the fail-closed test, and the T17 class sweep. Precision check: "records the signature on the FIRST approval" PASSES on the mutant - correct, because the guard only blocks the SECOND call, so the suite is discriminating rather than blunt. Model restored byte-identical after the mutation (diff clean); tsc --noEmit exit 0 and 27/27 green again. Independently CONFIRMED against real PostgreSQL 16 by an adversary tasked to refute it: the guarded UPDATE returns 0 against an approved row, and two concurrent transactions cannot both return 1 under READ COMMITTED (T2 blocks, re-evaluates its WHERE against the committed row via EvalPlanQual, reports 0).
items.notes: DESIGN DECISION - no-op returning 0, not a typed error. Justification: (1) re-approval is not a crash, and §21.1 requires a repeat run to be SAFE rather than loud ("a job may run more than once (retries, at-least-once delivery); design every job so a repeat run is safe") - a guarded UPDATE is precisely the idempotency guard that Article asks for - an at-least-once job, a retried request, or a double-clicked Approve button all re-issue the call and the desired end state already holds, so the idempotent answer is "0 rows changed", not an exception every caller must catch to behave correctly; (2) whether a 0 should surface as a 404, a 409, or a benign no-op is a POLICY question that depends on the calling surface, and this is a thin DB-correctness layer with no business logic in it (§6.1) - the model makes the illegal write impossible and reports what it did, the caller reads back with findByIdForOrg to decide what a 0 means; (3) returning 0 uniformly for wrong-org / no-such-id / already-approved preserves the no-existence-leak property (§11.7) that findByIdForOrg is deliberately built around. The guard is positive rather than `whereNot status = approved` so a future status ('revoked', 'archived') cannot transition to approved and mint a fresh signature - it fails closed until the predicate is deliberately widened. The guard is a single atomic UPDATE predicate rather than a read-then-write check, which would race: two concurrent approvals could both read 'draft' and both write. HONEST LIMIT ON CONCURRENCY: the suite asserts the draft-only predicate is IN the WHERE clause; it does NOT execute concurrent transactions - there is no live Postgres here (T18), so no test proves the two-writer race. The single guarded UPDATE is the right shape per Postgres's documented READ COMMITTED behaviour (a second UPDATE blocks on the first's row lock, re-evaluates its WHERE against the updated row on commit via EvalPlanQual, no longer matches status='draft', and reports 0; under REPEATABLE READ/SERIALIZABLE it raises a serialization failure instead - either way the signature is not overwritten, only the observable differs). That is REASONED from documented semantics, not observed, and is labelled as such in the code rather than claimed as a receipt.

items.title: THE CLASS: no entry point reachable on the model can reattribute a signature (§5.4/§11.7)
items.precondition: Guarding markApproved alone fixes the named instance. The class question is whether ANY callable path on this model can rewrite a finalized approved_by/approved_at - and whether a path added later would be caught. Every prior round of this PR failed by fixing only the reported instance, so the surface is enumerated at RUNTIME rather than trusted to a hand-written list.
steps: Enumerate every function-valued static reachable on TasteProfileModel by walking the prototype chain (own + inherited), and assert it equals a frozen, classified set.

Seed an APPROVED row and drive every candidate write against it - markApproved plus each sealed path called untyped, with a forged signature and the correct org/id - asserting the signature survives each one.
Mutation-check both halves: strip the markApproved guard; and separately add an unaudited write method to the class.
items.expected: The callable surface matches the audited classification exactly, and no reachable path leaves the row present-but-reattributed. A new or newly inherited entry point fails the suite until classified.
items.evidence: vitest exit 0 against the fixed model. THE SWEEP FOUND NO SECOND INSTANCE: enumerating the runtime surface yields 20 callables and every one is accounted for - 2 scoped readers (findByIdForOrg, findLatestByOrgAndLocation); 3 audited writers (create = draft-only + explicit column copy, markApproved = draft-only transition, deleteByIdForOrg = removes the row, and deletion is not reattribution); 8 sealed (count, createReturningId, deleteById, findById, findMany, findOne, paginate, updateById); 3 RAW ESCAPE HATCHES, explicitly NOT claimed as guarded (table, transaction, beginTransaction - `protected` is TypeScript-only, so `(Model as any).table()` returns a live knex builder at runtime and the transaction helpers hand back a handle that can write any table; a caller reaching past the model this way CAN reattribute a signature and no assertion pretends otherwise); 4 pure serialization internals (parseJson, toJson, serializeJsonFields, deserializeJsonFields - they transform values and never issue a query). markApproved was the only unsealed path that could rewrite a signature, and it is now guarded. MUTATION-VERIFIED both halves: (a) with the guard stripped, "no reachable entry point can alter an approved row's signature" fails; (b) adding a `static touchApproval()` that updates approved_by makes the surface test fail with `+ "touchApproval"` in the diff, exit 1 - proving a future unaudited write path cannot land silently. Model restored byte-identical after both mutations.
items.notes: This is the durable half of the fix: it converts "we swept the write paths once" into "the sweep is enforced on every run". The prior rounds' work is regression-covered rather than re-litigated - the sealed-entry-point tests, the draft-only create tests, and the forged-source/isRealSource fixes (T12/T13) all still pass unchanged, and the full-suite count only grew. The one honest gap this cannot close: a caller holding a raw knex/trx handle can write the table directly, bypassing the model entirely. That is true of every model in the repo and is out of this PR's control; the model is the enforcement boundary, not the database. CORRECTED AFTER ADVERSARIAL REVIEW: an independent adversary (running real PostgreSQL 16) refuted the first draft of this item's absolute claim. It showed that `table()` and `beginTransaction()` are one-call reattribution paths at runtime, while the classification called them 'internals'/'nonTable' that 'never query this table' - the methods themselves do not, but the handle they return does. They are now classified as raw escape hatches and the attack test states that it does not drive them, because driving them would FAIL: they genuinely can write. The provable claim is narrower and is the one now made: no TYPED caller can rewrite an existing row's signature. Also noted: the enumeration uses getOwnPropertyNames, so a Symbol-keyed static would be invisible to it (none exists today).

items.title: The taste_profiles migration runs (and rolls back) against a live PostgreSQL
items.precondition: RECORDED, NOT RUN. This PR ships a SQL migration (20260714000000_create_taste_profiles.ts), so migration execution is a real acceptance surface. It was missing from this artifact through rounds 1-3, which is itself a gap: the package asserted migration SAFETY while listing no item for actually executing it. Added now so the obligation is visible rather than implied. There is NO live database in this environment - no PostgreSQL, no Docker - so it cannot be executed here.
steps: Run `npm run db:migrate` against a PostgreSQL instance and confirm taste_profiles is created with its indexes.
Run `npm run db:rollback` and confirm the table is dropped cleanly.
Confirm the up() hasTable guard makes a re-run a no-op.
items.expected: Additive create with no locks on existing tables; idempotent up(); reversible down(). No existing table or row is touched.
items.evidence: NOT EXECUTED - no live PostgreSQL in this environment, so there is no execution receipt and none is claimed. What IS verified, by reading the migration: up() is guarded by `hasTable` and returns early if the table exists (idempotent); it only calls createTable on a NEW table, so it takes no lock on any existing table and rewrites no existing data; down() is a plain dropTableIfExists (reversible, and destructive only to the table this migration created). Risk to production data is therefore structural-only: a new empty table. This reading is NOT a substitute for a run and is not recorded as one.
items.notes: Per the waiver, the first real execution may occur during the dev deployment and must be checked through the deployment result, migration status, and DB health check (`/api/health/db`) - that check is the outstanding obligation this item hands to the deploy, not something this branch closed. ONE KNOWN STALENESS, recorded rather than silently carried: the migration's inline comment enumerates the status column as `// draft | approved`, and round 4 adds a third value, `superseded`. The comment was left untouched on purpose - editing it would make the file differ from the head this waiver names, trading a live owner waiver for a comment refresh that changes no SQL. The authoritative status set is the `TasteProfileStatus` union in TasteProfileModel.ts, which the migration's own note defers to ('an app-level enum'). Worth a one-line follow-up with no schema risk if the owner wants it aligned.
items.waiver: Owner waiver, Dave, PR #160 comment 2026-07-16T17:54:36Z, issued for head 18742ebe: "Dave explicitly waives a local/disposable PostgreSQL execution receipt as a pre-merge requirement... Pending live-PostgreSQL acceptance items may be marked waived with this reason." STILL GENUINELY APPLICABLE AT THIS HEAD, checked rather than assumed: `git diff 18742ebe -- src/database/migrations/` is EMPTY, so the migration is byte-identical to the head the waiver names. Rounds 3 and 4 changed TypeScript only. Round 4 in particular adds the `superseded` status WITHOUT a schema change - `status` is a plain text column with no CHECK constraint and the migration's own notes anticipate exactly this ('an app-level enum stored as text - a new status never needs a migration') - and it deliberately did NOT add the partial unique index discussed in T20's notes, precisely because that WOULD change the schema and void this waiver's basis. The waiver covers only the absence of a pre-merge database run; per its own terms it does not waive migration code review, tenant/data safety, rollback or data-loss concerns. The first real execution may occur during the dev deployment and must be checked through the deployment result, migration status, and the /api/health/db check - that is the outstanding obligation this item hands to the deploy.

items.title: No regression across the backend suite (round 3)
items.precondition: Node 22.23.1 in the /tmp/fix-160 worktree. Exit codes read directly from the command, never through a pipe (`$?` immediately after the process, with output redirected to a file) - a piped exit status reports the tail's, not the runner's. No live database, so DB-backed proofs stay pending (T7, T18).
steps: npx tsc --noEmit
npx vitest run
npm run check:conventions -- --strict
items.expected: All three gates green with no new violations attributable to this branch.
items.evidence: tsc --noEmit: exit 0. vitest (full suite): exit 0, 62 files / 507 tests passed, 0 failed - up from 499 at c337cb0a (+8 from this round's write-once, class-sweep and T20 gap-pinning tests); no previously passing test changed status. check:conventions -- --strict: exit 0, 'CLEAR structural violations - backend (size + console + db): 0'; no taste-profile file appears in any check. File sizes within §2.4 tiers after the change (model 313 lines, test 632). Prior rounds' verified work is intact and regression-covered: the 8 seals, draft-only create, and the resolveReviewSource/isRealSource honesty fixes all still pass unchanged.
items.notes: Local runs on Node 22.23.1, not CI - no pull_request workflow exists to attach checks to the PR head (T9). These are the same gates CI would have run.

items.title: delete + create + approve cannot erase a prior approval (append-only)
items.precondition: Found by an independent adversary tasked to refute the round-3 fix, then confirmed by the review (Dave, PR #160 comment 2026-07-16T20:51:46Z). The write-once guard made an approved ROW immutable but left the RECORD unprotected, because a record's identity here is org+location, not row id: deleteByIdForOrg() + create() + markApproved() - every call typed, tenant-scoped and individually legitimate - produced a new approved row signed by whoever ran it, with the previous signature gone entirely. Consumers read by org+location, so the org-level profile was re-signed with zero type violations. FIXED IN ROUND 4: approvals are append-only. deleteByIdForOrg() is now draft-only, so an approved or superseded row cannot be deleted through the model at all; markApproved() supersedes the incumbent for the same org+location in one transaction rather than anything being replaced or removed.
steps: Seed an approved org-level profile signed by owner A.
Call deleteByIdForOrg as owner B and assert it returns 0 and the row survives.
Call create + markApproved as owner B (the legitimate re-approval path).
Assert owner A's row is retired to status=superseded and KEEPS its own approved_by/approved_at.
Read back with findCurrentApprovedByOrgAndLocation and assert it resolves to owner B's new profile.
Assert both stakes are still present in the table - the table is its own approval ledger.
items.expected: The delete returns 0 and the approved row survives. Owner B does become the current approver - a real, staked, legitimate act that is ALLOWED - but owner A's signature survives on a superseded row with its original approver and timestamp. The threat was never 'someone else approves'; it was 'someone else approves and the previous stake disappears'. That is now impossible through the typed API.
items.evidence: Test "delete + create + approve CANNOT erase the original signature (T20)" in src/__tests__/TasteProfileModel.test.ts, plus the append-only suite ("TasteProfileModel - approvals are append-only (Sec 5.4)", 10 tests: approved not deletable, superseded not deletable, draft IS deletable, supersession retires-not-replaces, supersession does not cross locations, supersession does not cross organizations, the consumer read never serves a draft or a superseded row or another org's row, and a full two-approval lifecycle leaves every stake on the record). Node 22.23.1: `npx vitest run src/__tests__/TasteProfileModel.test.ts` -> exit 0, 37/37 passed. `npx tsc --noEmit` -> exit 0. Full suite `npx vitest run` -> exit 0, 517/517 across 62 files. NON-VACUOUS: see T22 - removing the guard makes this test fail.
items.notes: DESIGN, and why this shape. Of the three options recorded in round 3 (refuse to delete an approved profile / supersede rather than delete / append-only audit table), this implements the first two together and deliberately REJECTS the third. The rows themselves are the ledger: nothing approved is ever deleted, nothing approved is ever mutated except the one-way retire-to-superseded, and every row keeps the person who staked it and when - so the rows for an org+location ordered by approved_at ARE the approval history. A separate audit table was rejected because (a) it OBSERVES a deletion rather than PREVENTING it - it would have narrated this exact laundering instead of blocking it, which is not what the review asked for, and (b) it is a second write recording the same fact, which can drift from the first, making the audit itself a claim without a receipt. The record is the row. NO SCHEMA CHANGE WAS NEEDED: `superseded` is a new value in an app-level text enum, which the migration's own notes anticipate ('a new status never needs a migration'), and the table has no CHECK constraint (T21). That keeps the migration byte-identical to the head Dave's live-PostgreSQL waiver names (T18). BOUND ON THIS PROOF, stated rather than implied away: the suite runs against an in-memory table with no locks, no isolation and no real transaction, so it proves the SEQUENTIAL exploit is closed - which is exactly how the adversary executed it - and proves nothing about concurrency. Sequentially, at most one row per org+location is approved. Two CONCURRENT approvals of two DIFFERENT drafts for the same org+location can still both commit, leaving two approved rows: a resolution ambiguity, NOT a lost signature (nothing is deleted, nothing is reattributed, and both rows carry a real human's stake). That is strictly NARROWER than the pre-fix behaviour, where two approved rows could also arise sequentially because nothing retired an incumbent. Closing it for real is a partial unique index on (organization_id, location_id) WHERE status='approved' - and it needs TWO indexes, since Postgres treats NULLs as distinct and location_id is nullable. That is a schema change with no live database here to execute or verify it against, and it would void the byte-identical basis of T18's waiver, so it is documented in the model's class docstring and handed to the wiring PR rather than guessed at now.

items.title: HONEST LIMIT: write-once is a model convention, not a database constraint
items.precondition: Recorded so the guarantee's boundary is explicit. The draft-only transition is enforced in the model's WHERE clause. It is NOT enforced by the database.
steps: Inspect the migration for CHECK constraints or triggers on status/approved_by/approved_at.
Confirm what a raw-SQL or raw-knex writer can do.
items.expected: The model is the enforcement boundary for typed callers; a raw writer is not constrained by it.
items.evidence: Verified by the adversary against real PostgreSQL 16: taste_profiles has NO CHECK constraint - the primary key is its only constraint - so `status` can physically hold any text (e.g. 'archived') via raw SQL, and approved_by/approved_at can be rewritten by any raw writer. This is why the model's guard is POSITIVE (status = draft): it fails closed against exactly such a value rather than transitioning it to approved. Consistent with the migration's own note that status is 'an app-level enum stored as text... a new status never needs a migration'.
items.notes: Not closed here, and NOT closable here: a DB-level CHECK or an approval-immutability trigger is a schema change with its own production-risk review, and there is no live PostgreSQL in this environment to execute or verify one against - so it could only ever be shipped unverified. It is also NOT what the review asked for: Dave asked for approvals to be append-only at the model layer (2026-07-16T20:51:46Z), which round 4 delivers (T20). `(Model as any).table()` and the transaction helpers are the in-process form of the same limit - true of every model in this repo, not specific to this table. Round 4 does NOT narrow this limit and does not claim to: a raw writer can still delete an approved row or rewrite a signature, because the guard is a WHERE clause in the model, not a constraint in the database. Recorded in the model's class docstring as bound 2 so no reader mistakes the model boundary for a database boundary.
items.waiver: WAIVED as an accepted, recorded limit on Dave's explicit round-4 authorization (PR #160 comment 2026-07-16T20:51:46Z): "roll `test-results.json` to Passed or record an explicit accepted waiver", issued against the open T20/T21 pair. This is the T21 half. It is one: the model is the enforcement boundary for TYPED callers and always was, which is true of every model in this repo; hardening the DATABASE against a raw writer is a separate schema change with its own production-risk review, and no live PostgreSQL exists here to execute or verify a CHECK constraint against. NOT covered by Dave's live-PostgreSQL waiver (2026-07-16T17:54:36Z) and that waiver is deliberately NOT claimed here: it waives the absence of a pre-merge database RUN, whereas T21 is the absence of a database CONSTRAINT - a design decision, not a missing execution. Citing it here would be a misuse of it. Waived deliberately, NOT converted to pass: nothing in this branch proves a DB-level constraint exists, because none does, and this artifact does not claim otherwise.

items.title: The append-only guarantee is non-vacuous (mutation-tested)
items.precondition: A passing test proves nothing until it is shown it CAN fail. T20's whole value is that it fails when the guard is gone, so the guard was reverted and the suite re-run against the unguarded model.
steps: Remove `status: "draft"` from the WHERE clause of deleteByIdForOrg - i.e. restore the exact pre-fix delete.
Run `npx vitest run src/__tests__/TasteProfileModel.test.ts` and read the process exit code directly.
Restore the guard and re-run.
items.expected: With the guard removed the suite FAILS - T20 in particular - because the approved row is deleted and the original signature is destroyed. With the guard restored it passes.
items.evidence: Node 22.23.1, exit codes read directly from the process (not through a pipe). GUARD REMOVED: `npx vitest run src/__tests__/TasteProfileModel.test.ts` -> exit 1, "Tests 4 failed | 33 passed (37)". The 4 failures are exactly the append-only assertions: "an approved profile cannot be deleted", "a superseded profile cannot be deleted either - history is not disposable", "delete + create + approve CANNOT erase the original signature (T20)", and "no reachable entry point can alter an approved row's signature". T20's failure reads `AssertionError: expected 1 to be +0` - the unguarded DELETE reports 1 row destroyed where the guarded one must report 0. GUARD RESTORED: same command -> exit 0, 37/37 passed.
items.notes: This is the receipt behind T20, kept as its own item because "the tests pass" is not evidence that the fix does anything - only a seen failure is. Same discipline as T5 for the isolation suite. Note what it does NOT show: the in-memory harness has no locks, no isolation and no real transaction, so this proves the SEQUENTIAL exploit is closed (the way the adversary actually executed it) and says nothing about concurrent approvals - see T20's notes for that bound.

items.title: No regression across the backend suite (round 4)
items.precondition: Round 4 changes a model's delete predicate, adds a transaction to markApproved, adds a status to the lifecycle enum, and extends the mocked data layer. Everything the earlier rounds verified must still hold - in particular the one-way markApproved guard, the draft-only create, all 8 sealed BaseModel entry points, the forged-source fix in resolveReviewSource, the isRealSource allowlist, and the runtime-surface guard test.
steps: Run `npx tsc --noEmit`.
Run `npx vitest run` across the whole backend suite.
Run `npm run check:conventions -- --strict`.
Read every exit code directly from the process, not through a pipe.
items.expected: All three gates pass with exit 0, and no previously passing test regresses.
items.evidence: Node 22.23.1, exit codes read directly. `npx tsc --noEmit` -> exit 0. `npx vitest run` -> exit 0, "Test Files 62 passed (62), Tests 517 passed (517)". `npm run check:conventions -- --strict` -> exit 0, "CLEAR structural violations - backend (size + console + db): 0". The runtime-surface guard did its job during this round: adding findCurrentApprovedByOrgAndLocation failed the "has no callable static beyond the audited set" test until the new reader was explicitly classified - which is the behaviour that test exists for.
items.notes: Baseline for comparison, captured on the unmodified head c173e110 before any round-4 edit: `npx tsc --noEmit` -> exit 0; `npx vitest run` on the two taste-profile files -> exit 0, 51/51. After round 4 the same two files run 63/63 (37 in TasteProfileModel.test.ts, up from 25) with no test removed - only the T20 assertion inverted, from pinning the gap as current behaviour to proving it closed.
